title: Havel's Problem
statement_text: Does there exist a constant $d$ such that every planar graph in which every two distinct triangles are at distance at least $d$ is 3-colorable?
context_text: Grünbaum conjectured that every planar graph with no intersecting triangles is 3-colorable, but this was disproved by Havel, who then formulated this more cautious question. Havel showed that if such a constant exists then $d \geq 3$, and Aksionov and Mel'nikov later improved this lower bound to $d \geq 4$.
paper_contribution: Proves a positive answer (Theorem 1.2): there exists an absolute constant $d$ such that every planar graph with every two distinct triangles at distance at least $d$ is 3-colorable; the proof yields an explicit but very large upper bound of roughly $10^{100}$.
paper_title: Three-coloring triangle-free graphs on surfaces V. Coloring planar graphs with distant anomalies

title: Thomassen's Conjecture (exponentially many 3-colorings)
statement_text: Every triangle-free planar graph has exponentially many 3-colorings.
context_text: Thomassen conjectured that all triangle-free planar graphs have exponentially many 3-colorings. The paper resolves a special case via Corollary 1.6 by prescribing colors to a large independent set of vertices guaranteed by bounded maximum degree.
paper_contribution: Proves a special case (Corollary 1.6): for every $k \geq 0$ there exists $c > 1$ such that every planar triangle-free graph $G$ of maximum degree at most $k$ and without separating 4-cycles has at least $c^{|V(G)|}$ distinct 3-colorings.
notes: The paper notes that once Conjecture 1.5 was confirmed by Dvořák and Lidický, the assumption of no separating 4-cycles in Corollary 1.6 can be dropped.
paper_title: Three-coloring triangle-free graphs on surfaces V. Coloring planar graphs with distant anomalies

title: Conjecture 1.4
statement_text: There exists an absolute constant $d \geq 2$ with the following property. Let $G$ be a plane triangle-free graph, let $S$ be a set of vertices of $G$ and let $\psi : S \to \{1, 2, 3\}$ be an arbitrary function. If the distance between every two vertices of $S$ is at least $d$, then $\psi$ extends to a 3-coloring of $G$.
context_text: This is a natural extension of Theorem 1.3 to the case of precolored single vertices, where forbidding separating 4-cycles should not be necessary. The paper shows in Theorem 5.1 that Conjecture 1.4 is implied by the seemingly simpler Conjecture 1.5.
notes: The paper states that Conjecture 1.5 was subsequently confirmed by Dvořák and Lidický [16], which implies Conjecture 1.4 is also true.
paper_title: Three-coloring triangle-free graphs on surfaces V. Coloring planar graphs with distant anomalies

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: There exists an absolute constant $d \geq 2$ with the following property. Let $G$ be a plane triangle-free graph, let $C$ be a 4-cycle bounding a face of $G$ and let $v$ be a vertex of $G$. Let $\psi$ be a 3-coloring of $C + v$. If the distance between $C$ and $v$ is at least $d$, then $\psi$ extends to a 3-coloring of $G$.
context_text: This statement, concerning the extendability of a precoloring of a facial 4-cycle together with a single distant vertex, is shown in Theorem 5.1 to imply Conjecture 1.4, making it a key reduction in the authors' framework.
notes: The paper explicitly states that Conjecture 1.5 was confirmed by Dvořák and Lidický [16] while the paper was under review.
paper_title: Three-coloring triangle-free graphs on surfaces V. Coloring planar graphs with distant anomalies
